A homomorphism from F to G is a map h : V (F ) → V (G) such that {u, v} ∈ E(F ) implies {h(u), h(v)} ∈ E(G). A subgraph isomorphism is an injective homomorphism.
A homomorphism from F to G is a edge-preserving map. A subgraph isomorphism ensures that F actually occurs as a subgraph of G, mapping distinct edges to distinct edges.
Homomorphisms from F to G are related to cardinality by hom(F, G) and sub(F, G) respectively
Subgraph isomorphisms from F to G are related to cardinality by sub(F, G)

A graph invariant ζ can homomorphism-count F if for all pairs G, H ∈ G ζ(G) = ζ(H) implies hom(F, G) = hom(F, H).
By analogy, ζ can subgraph-count F if for all pairs G, H ∈ G, ζ(G) = ζ(H) implies sub(F, G) = sub(F, H).
